examples.german: Mein Vorname ist Thomas und mein Familienname ist Müller.
examples.english: My first name is Thomas and my last name is Müller.
examples.note: Uses: der Vorname, der Familienname. Possessive pronoun 'mein' with masculine nouns.

examples.german: Das Alphabet hat 26 Buchstaben auf Deutsch.
examples.english: The alphabet has 26 letters in German.
examples.note: Uses: das Alphabet (neuter, borrowed word → often das).

examples.german: Wie ist Ihr Name, bitte? — Mein Name ist Schmidt.
examples.english: What is your name, please? — My name is Schmidt.
examples.note: Uses: der Name. Formal address with 'Ihr'.

examples.german: Die Information ist sehr wichtig für das Gespräch.
examples.english: The information is very important for the conversation.
examples.note: Uses: die Information (-tion → always die), das Gespräch. Demonstrates both nouns in one sentence.

examples.german: Herr Meier kommt aus Deutschland und lernt Musik.
examples.english: Mr. Meier comes from Germany and learns music.
examples.note: Uses: der Herr, die Musik (singular-only noun). Verb 'kommen' and 'lernen' from Lektion 1.

examples.german: Frau Fischer hat eine Münze in der Hand.
examples.english: Ms. Fischer has a coin in her hand.
examples.note: Uses: die Frau, die Münze. Introducing the concept of accusative with 'eine'.

examples.german: Das Land heißt Deutschland und die Hauptstadt ist Berlin.
examples.english: The country is called Germany and the capital is Berlin.
examples.note: Uses: das Land. Verb 'heißen' from Lektion 1 vocabulary.

examples.german: Der Test ist heute — buchstabieren Sie Ihren Namen, bitte.
examples.english: The test is today — please spell your name.
examples.note: Uses: der Test, der Name. Verb 'buchstabieren' from Lektion 1.

examples.german: Die Persönlichkeit aus Südafrika spricht Deutsch.
examples.english: The celebrity from South Africa speaks German.
examples.note: Uses: die Persönlichkeit (-keit → always die). Country reference from Lektion 1.

examples.german: Das Gespräch mit Herrn Müller ist sehr interessant.
examples.english: The conversation with Mr. Müller is very interesting.
examples.note: Uses: das Gespräch, der Herr (dative: Herrn). Formal address pattern.

exercises.explanation: 'Name' is masculine (der Name). Must memorize — no reliable pattern.
Formal possessive 'Ihr' (your) is used with 'Vorname' (masculine). 'Ihr' stays unchanged in nominative.
'Alphabet' is neuter (das Alphabet). It is a borrowed word — often neuter.
'Information' ends in -tion → always feminine (die). High reliability pattern.
'Frau' is used as a title of address for women. die Frau → used before surname.
'Gespräch' is neuter (das Gespräch). Must memorize.
'Münze' is feminine (die Münze). Must memorize.
'Test' is masculine (der Test), despite being a borrowed word. Must memorize.

exercises.prompt: Mein ___ ist Müller und mein ___ ist Klaus. (last name / first name)
exercises.explanation: Both are masculine nouns. 'Familienname' = last name, 'Vorname' = first name.

exercises.prompt: ___ Musik aus Deutschland ist sehr gut. (the music)
exercises.explanation: 'Musik' is feminine (die Musik). It is a singular-only noun — no plural form.
'Land' is neuter (das Land). Plural: die Länder (umlaut + -er).

exercises.prompt: Herr Braun kommt aus ___. Das ist ein großes ___. (a country → Deutschland)
exercises.explanation: 'Land' is neuter. Country names like Deutschland are not used with an article normally, but 'Land' as a common noun needs 'das'.
'Name' is masculine (der Name). 'Die' is feminine, 'Das' is neuter — both wrong here.
'Information' ends in -tion → always feminine (die). High reliability rule.
'Gespräch' is neuter (das Gespräch). Must memorize.
Nouns ending in -keit/-heit form their plural with -en: die Persönlichkeit → die Persönlichkeiten.
'Land' forms its plural with umlaut + -er: das Land → die Länder.
'Münze' is feminine. The indefinite article in accusative for feminine nouns is 'eine'.

exercises.prompt: Das ist ___ Herr Kaufmann. Er kommt aus der Schweiz.
exercises.explanation: As a title of address before a surname, 'Herr' is used without an article.
'Musik' is feminine (die Musik). It has no plural form (Sg. only).

exercises.solution: Wie ist Ihr Vorname? / Wie heißt du? (informal)
exercises.explanation: Use 'Vorname' for 'first name'. Formal: 'Ihr Vorname'. Informal: 'dein Vorname' or 'Wie heißt du?'
'Test' is masculine (der Test). 'heute' = today.
'Gespräch' is neuter (das Gespräch). 'sehr' intensifies the adjective.
'Land' is neuter → indefinite article 'ein'. No article before country names like Deutschland.

exercises.prompt: Mr. Braun comes from Germany. His last name is Braun and his first name is Thomas.
exercises.solution: Herr Braun kommt aus Deutschland. Sein Familienname ist Braun und sein Vorname ist Thomas.
exercises.explanation: Uses der Herr (title, no article before surname), der Familienname, der Vorname. Possessive 'sein' (his) for masculine nouns.

exercises.prompt: The information about the conversation is important.
exercises.solution: Die Information über das Gespräch ist wichtig.
exercises.explanation: die Information (-tion → die); das Gespräch (neuter). Preposition 'über' + accusative.
'Musik' is feminine and used without article here (like English). 'sehr gern' = very much/gladly.
'Name' is masculine (der Name), not feminine. Must memorize this gender.
'Information' ends in -tion → always feminine (die). High reliability rule.
'Gespräch' is neuter (das Gespräch). Must memorize.
'Persönlichkeit' ends in -keit → always feminine (die). High reliability rule.
Plural of 'Land' is 'Länder' (umlaut ä + er), not 'Lander'.

exercises.prompt: Herr Fischer kommt aus das Land Schweiz.
exercises.solution: Herr Fischer kommt aus der Schweiz.
exercises.explanation: 'die Schweiz' is feminine. After 'aus', the dative is used: 'aus der Schweiz'. Country names that use an article require the dative form.

exercises.prompt: Give the plural of: der Name
exercises.explanation: 'Name' adds -n in the plural: der Name → die Namen.

exercises.prompt: Give the plural of: die Frau
exercises.explanation: 'Frau' adds -en in the plural: die Frau → die Frauen.

exercises.prompt: Give the plural of: das Gespräch
exercises.explanation: 'Gespräch' adds -e in the plural: das Gespräch → die Gespräche.

exercises.prompt: Give the plural of: das Land
exercises.explanation: 'Land' uses umlaut + -er: das Land → die Länder.

exercises.prompt: Give the plural of: die Information
exercises.explanation: 'Information' adds -en: die Information → die Informationen.

exercises.prompt: Give the plural of: der Herr
exercises.explanation: 'Herr' adds -en in the plural: der Herr → die Herren. Note the doubled 'r'.

exercises.prompt: Give the plural of: die Persönlichkeit
exercises.explanation: Nouns in -keit/-heit always form their plural with -en: die Persönlichkeit → die Persönlichkeiten.

exercises.prompt: Give the plural of: der Test
exercises.explanation: 'Test' is a borrowed word and adds -s in the plural: der Test → die Tests.

exercises.prompt: Give the plural of: die Münze
exercises.explanation: 'Münze' adds -n in the plural: die Münze → die Münzen.

exercises.prompt: Wie buchstabieren Sie Ihren ___? — Mein ___ ist K-A-U-F-M-A-N-N. (last name / last name)
exercises.explanation: After 'Ihren' (accusative), 'Familiennamen' is the accusative form. After 'Mein' (nominative), 'Familienname' stays in nominative.

exercises.prompt: Das ist ___ Herr Schmidt. Er ist eine bekannte ___. (Mr. / personality)
exercises.explanation: 'Herr' as title of address needs no article before surname. 'Persönlichkeit' is feminine (-keit).
